outline.title: Introduction and Scope
outline.objective: Define the review scope covering pedestrian behavior modeling at signalized intersections and unsignalized crosswalks, establishing the comparative framework for examining methodological approaches and claim conflicts

outline.title: Microscopic Simulation Models for Pedestrian Behavior
outline.objective: Synthesize microscopic simulation approaches including agent-based force models and pedestrian type classification systems, examining their applicability to signal compliance and crossing behavior

outline.title: Statistical and Econometric Models for Crossing Behavior
outline.objective: Compare statistical mixture models, random parameter logit models, and discrete choice models for analyzing pedestrian waiting times, red light violation propensity, and speed change events

outline.title: Probabilistic and Machine Learning Approaches
outline.objective: Evaluate Dynamic Bayesian Network models with particle filtering for pedestrian crossing decision recognition and motion classification, comparing with regression-based prediction methods

outline.title: Experimental and Field Study Methods
outline.objective: Review VR-based experiments for autonomous vehicle-pedestrian interaction, field experiments on mobile phone distraction effects, and video observation methodologies for behavior analysis

outline.title: Geographic Context and Cross-Cultural Applicability
outline.objective: Examine the geographic distribution of study sites (China, Japan, Hong Kong, India), assess generalizability claims, and address the verified gap regarding Chinese-context representation

outline.title: Claim Conflicts and Methodological Discrepancies
outline.objective: Analyze the 41 documented contradictions across papers including metric inconsistencies (MAPE vs modified_rho_squared), parameter value differences (r2=0.69 vs 0.35), and conflicting findings on waiting time distributions

outline.title: Synthesis and Recommendations
outline.objective: Provide integrated findings across methodological families, identify gaps in current research, and propose standardized comparison criteria for future pedestrian behavior studies

sections.title: Introduction and Scope
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 13 papers examining Safety analysis, Risk assessment, Human reliability analysis, with findings organized around 59 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Risk Assessment / Safety Analysis Methods, microscopic traffic simulation, agent based force based microscopic simulation as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (2 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Microscopic Simulation Models for Pedestrian Behavior
sections.text: This section synthesizes findings from 13 papers examining Safety analysis, Risk assessment, Human reliability analysis using Risk Assessment / Safety Analysis Methods, microscopic traffic simulation, agent based force based microscopic simulation. The comparative evidence matrix structures claims, metrics, and limitations to support structured analysis. A key observation is that Risk Assessment / Safety Analysis Methods and microscopic traffic simulation approaches are the dominant methodological families in this corpus, though significant heterogeneity in reporting practices limits direct cross-study comparison.

sections.title: Statistical and Econometric Models for Crossing Behavior
sections.text: Reported metrics in the reviewed literature span multiple dimensions of crossing behavior and traffic system performance, including: proportion of red light jumpers; proportion of violation followers; pedestrian arriving interval distribution; gap acceptance (ΔT) distribution, Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for position: 5.7%; Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) for speed: 10.5%; Passing position distribution at three cross-sections (t-test); Lane-formation number comparison; Passing time distribution, proportion of red light jumpers; proportion of violation followers; DT (time gap acceptance criterion), feature rating 7point scale; crossing time seconds; waiting time seconds; receptivity score; personal innovativeness score, CMIN/DF ratio; RMSEA; P-close; CFI; regression weights; Cronbach alpha. Performance reporting is most complete for Safety analysis tasks. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers in two key respects. First, normalization approaches differ: some studies express outcomes as normalized rates per unit time or per pedestrian, while others report raw counts. Second, the temporal resolution of reported metrics ranges from instantaneous (e.g., crossing initiation delay at a single phase) to aggregate (e.g., violation frequency over a multi-hour observation window). These differences make direct cross-study comparison difficult without explicit standardization. Addressing this heterogeneity will require consensus on metric definitions and reporting standards within the pedestrian behavior modeling community. The absence of such standards currently forces reviewers to rely on narrative synthesis rather than formal meta-analysis, limiting the precision of conclusions that can be drawn.

sections.title: Probabilistic and Machine Learning Approaches
sections.text: The Risk Assessment / Safety Analysis Methods family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The microscopic traffic simulation family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The agent based force based microscopic simulation family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing pedestrian-vehicle interaction patterns. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different group compositions. The microscopic traffic simulation behavioral model family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The experimental simulation family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 13 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Synthesis and Recommendations
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 13 papers reveals 2 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [dc87fdd8] (medium severity): Current coverage appears to underrepresent Chinese-language or Chinese-context studies.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [e200c51a] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under the same task, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.
